judgments.comparative_verdict: Report 1 answered better: it correctly identifies that the GitHub PR (three-dot) diff re-includes scorer.py, which Report 2 misses by focusing on the clean two-dot diff.

report_1.evidence: **`main`.** The branch was originally cut from the in-progress `feat/weighted-scorer`, so the naive base would be that branch. But the weighted-scorer work has already landed on `main`... so the PR should target `main`.

report_2.evidence: The apparent alternative — stacking on `feat/weighted-scorer` (the dependency this branch was built on) — is exactly the dead-base trap: that work has already landed in `main` via PR #42 ... The dependency branch is superseded, so the PR must target `main`.

report_1.evidence: the dependency has effectively landed on `main`, but as a *rewritten* single commit (`1971f08`, #42), not by merging the original W1–W3 commits. So `main` already contains all of the weighted-scorer content under different commit IDs.

report_2.evidence: its commits `d6fdbc3/2d3029b/ac9d505` are not ancestors of `main` (the merge base is only `65204ad`). But its *content* has landed in `main` independently: PR #42's single commit `1971f08` produced identical results ... the weighted work was re-landed as a squashed/refactored commit with a *different SHA*

report_1.evidence: The rest — `overlap()` in `metrics.py` and the whole `scorer.py` weighted rewrite — is the **`feat/weighted-scorer` dependency (W1–W3)**. It shows up in the three-dot diff because the branch was cut from `feat/weighted-scorer` *before* that work reached `main`

`git rebase --onto main ac9d505 feat/pairwise-metric` ... This replays **only X1 (`7b2e100`) and X2 (`1bfecb6`)** onto `main`, discarding W1–W3. ... **Verify the result:** `git log --oneline main..feat/pairwise-metric` should show only `X1`/`X2`, and `git diff main...feat/pairwise-metric` should now equal the clean 5-line pairwise-only change

report_2.evidence: `git rebase --onto origin/main feat/weighted-scorer feat/pairwise-metric`. This replays *only* X1 (`7b2e100`) and X2 (`1bfecb6`) on top of `main`, discarding W1–W3 ... `git log origin/main..HEAD` should show only X1/X2, and `git diff origin/main..HEAD` should show only the two pairwise changes.
